summary: DHH argues that email tracking pixels are a form of spying that violates basic privacy expectations and should be eliminated by default in mailing list software. He shares that Basecamp stopped using tracking pixels months earlier with no negative consequences. He contends that regardless of their marketing utility, spy pixels are invasive, non-consensual, and violate what normal people expect from email. He proposes two simple reforms: mailing list software should disable tracking pixels by default, and senders who want open tracking should obtain explicit opt-in consent from recipients.
key_points: Most normal people are shocked to learn that simply opening an email can trigger tracking, violating basic expectations about email privacy
Basecamp stopped using tracking pixels and found it was actually a relief, proving they aren't necessary
The dictionary definition of 'spy' fits email tracking pixels perfectly: secretly collecting and reporting information without knowledge or consent
The reform is simple: disable tracking by default and require explicit opt-in consent when senders want open tracking
Link tracking with URL parameters is an acceptable alternative since clicking links is an explicit action users understand involves some tracking
money_quotes: The internet is finally coming out of its long haze on privacy, but it's with one hell of a hangover.
Not being able to track open rates, and fret over whether that meant our subject lines weren't providing just the right HOOK, has actually been a relief.
They're invasive, they're extracted without consent, and they break the basic assumptions most people have about email.
Just about every normal person (i.e. someone not working in internet marketing) has been surprised, pissed, or at least dismayed when I tell them about spy pixels in emails.
A lot of them have taken offense to the term 'spy pixels'. Affixing the spying label made a lot of them uncomfortable, because they were just trying to help!
Here's the dictionary definition of a spy: 'a person who secretly collects and reports information on the activities, movements, and plans'. That fits pretty well to a spy pixel.
Don't do it by default, ask for informed consent if you must. Being respectful of someone's privacy isn't rocket science.
And if we keep making things a little better, we'll eventually wake up to a world that's a lot better.
key_insight: Email tracking pixels meet the literal definition of spying and should require explicit opt-in consent rather than being silently enabled by default in mailing list software.
